content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


April 21, 2005


Elizabeth Wagner Hull
Assistant City Attorney
City of Chula Vista
276 Fourth Avenue
Chula Vista, CA 91910


Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-05-057

Dear Ms. Wagner:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Councilmember Jerry
Rindone of the City of Chula Vista, for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Councilmember Rindone participate in his official capacity, in community
meetings, informational sessions and/or city council workshops or hearings regarding the
city's general plan update?

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the governmental decisions
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Rindone's
private residence. Therefore, unless this presumption is rebutted, or an exception under
regulation 18702.4 applies, the council member is disqualified from participating in the
general plan decisions.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are seeking advice regarding recent amendments to regulation 18704.2
regarding the general plan adoption or amendments.

In a phone call, you referred to an earlier advice letter you received, Full Advice
Letter A-03-280, dated January 6, 2004. This letter concluded that Councilmember Jerry
Rindone was presumed to be materially affected by the proposed changes to the land use
element of the city's general plan. The letter concluded that unless rebutted, the council
member was disqualified from participating in the decision.

Since that letter was written, the Commission has amended regulation 18704.2.
You requested that facts from your earlier letter be incorporated in your current request
for advice. You stated that none of the facts have changed.

In your earlier letter, you said that Councilmember Rindone resides on the west
side of the city and the city is evaluating the existing land use designations. The
general plan proposes no change to his primary residence. However, the plan
does propose possible changes to properties within 500 feet of it. The possible changes
vary among three different land use concepts. One of the proposed concept plans will
affect approximately 9 properties within that radius. The concepts are still very broad
and it is too early in the process to more specifically identify the impacts or the number
of parcels citywide subject to the same types of redesignation. There are three concepts
under consideration:

Concept A: Change from current "Retail Commercial" to proposed "Mixed Use
Commercial" for 4 properties. The Mixed Use Commercial land would allow for 40%
offices and 60% retail.

Concept B: Change from current "Retail Commercial," "High Density
Residential (18-27 du/ac)" and "Professional and Administrative Commercial" to
proposed "Mixed Use." This change affects 9 lots within the 500 foot radius. This
Mixed Use would allow 40% developed as residential (40 du/ac), 40% as office and 20%
as retail.

Concept C: Change from current "Retail Commercial," "High Density
Residential (18-27 du/ac)" and "Professional and Administrative Commercial" to
proposed "Mixed Use." This change affects 9 lots within the 500 foot radius. This
Mixed Use would allow 40% developed as residential (40 du/ac), 50% as office and 10%
as retail.

Over the next few months, the city will hold a series of workshops and council
hearings on the vision and themes of the general plan, the community's key issues, and
the content of each of the general plan elements.

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 3

At the time of the original advice letter the general plan update was only at the
land use concept stage. No determination had been made regarding the exact changes in
land use designation or all of the properties that may be impacted. The council will be
discussing the proposed changes to the land use and other elements. The consideration
for approval of each element will be at a hearing probably in July 2005. The workshops
and council hearings prior to that will take comment and generate discussion of each
section of the general plan. Also once the general plan is adopted numerous rezones and
other actions will be required before the general plan update can be fully implemented.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest Prohibition

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official
has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family" or on any of
the official's economic interests. To determine whether a public official has such a
"conflict of interest" in a governmental decision, the Commission has developed a
standard, eight-step analysis outlined at subdivisions 1 through 8 of regulation 18700(b).

1. & 2. Is Councilmember Jerry Rindone considered a "public official" and
will he be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

As a member of the Chula Vista City Council, Jerry Rindone is a "member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency" and is, therefore, a
public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).)

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1.)

Councilmember Rindone will "make a governmental decision" if he votes on a
general plan amendment decision. In addition to actually voting on these matters, if he
engages in negotiations without significant substantive review, provides advice or makes
recommendations, he will be "participating" in a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) He
will also be "influencing" that decision if he appears before or otherwise attempts to

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 4

influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. (Regulation
18702.3.)²

Exceptions:

The only possible exceptions that may apply are found in regulation
18702.4(b)(1)(A-C). Even if a conflict of interest is present, a public official may appear
before his or her agency as a member of the general public in the course of its prescribed
governmental function in order to represent himself or herself, but only in regard to
matters related solely to his or her personal interests, including, but not limited to:

"(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate family.
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members
of his or her immediate family.
(C) A business entity over which the official exercises sole
direction and control, or over which the official and his or her spouse
jointly exercise sole direction and control." (Ibid.)

Under the facts that you provide, Councilmember Rindone may appear as a
member of the general public at community meetings, informational sessions and/or city
council study sessions or hearings regarding the general plan update so long as he is solely
representing his own personal interests. This is a very narrow exception and it does not
permit him to address the interests of other property or business owners.

3. What are Councilmember Rindone's economic interests — the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

•	A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment³ of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);

² If a public official enumerated in section 87200 (including city council members) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as the details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) Since Mr. Rindone is a member of the city council, a position enumerated in section 87200, these requirements apply to him.

³ A public official has a "direct or indirect investment" of $2,000 or more in a business entity, or owns a real property interest, as defined in section 87103(d).

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 5

regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1(b));

•

A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);

•

A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

•

A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

•

A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

Councilmember Rindone has an economic interest in real property which serves
as his principal residence, provided he has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 in his
property.

You have not provided information regarding any other economic interest of
Councilmember Rindone. For purposes of this letter, we assume that he has no other
economic interests relevant to the decision you have identified.

4. Are the councilmember's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?

Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if, among other things:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or
any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision." (Regulation
18704.2(a)(1).)

3 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 6

You have stated that the council member's residence is not located in an area which would be subject to any proposed general plan change. However, Councilmember Rindone's residence is located within 500 feet of other properties that would be subject to the proposed changes. Because each of these nearby properties would be subject to land use designation changes, subdivision (a)(1) of regulation 18704.2 above will apply so that the official's properties are considered "directly involved."

Subdivision (b) of this regulation provides several exceptions to the direct involvement provisions of subdivision (a).

General Plan Exception:

There is a very narrow exception in regulation 18704.2(b)(3), which provides that real property in which a public official has an interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, but is instead indirectly involved, if the decision solely concerns the adoption or amendment of a general plan and all of the following apply:

(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise exclusively one of policy. A decision will not qualify under this subdivision if the decision is initiated by the public official, by a person that is an economic interest of the public official, or by a person representing either the public official or an economic interest of the public official.

(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objectives. Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting, licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.

(C) The decision does not concern an identifiable parcel or parcels or development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel or parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which the decision is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area depicted on a map or diagram offered in connection with the decision, provided that the map or diagram depicts all parcels located within the agency's jurisdiction and economic interests of the official are not singled out.

(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior concurrent or subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license, zoning designation, zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.

According to your facts, the city's general plan update merely identifies planning objectives or is otherwise solely one of policy. You also stated in a telephone conversation on April 12, 2005 that the decision to update the general plan was not

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 7

initiated by Councilmember Rindone, nor any person that is an economic interest of his.
Nor was it initiated by a person representing Councilmember Rindone or an economic
interest of his.

Furthermore, you said that the general plan update would require further decisions
by the city council such as numerous rezoning determinations that will be necessary after
the plan is adopted. Lastly, you stated that the decisions also do not concern identifiable
parcels of land as the land use concepts (A-C) described in your earlier letter of
November 17, 2003, were general in nature and not specific. You also said in the
telephone call that the general plan update does not concern the agency's prior,
concurrent or subsequent approval of or change to, a permit license, zoning designation,
zoning variance, land use ordinance, specific plan or its equivalent.

However, based on the information provided in your earlier letter, it appears that
the general plan proposed changes (Concepts A, B, and C) to properties within 500 feet
of Councilmember Rindone's primary residence, relate to identifiable parcels of land. In
your previous letter, you included a map which designates specific areas for the proposed
changes. Furthermore, you stated in that letter that Concept A, which would allow for
40% offices and 60% retail development, would affect 4 properties within the 500 foot
radius. Concept B, which would allow 40% development as residential, 40% as office
and 20% as retail, would affect 9 lots; and Concept C, would allow development of 40%
residential, 50% office and 10% retail, would affect 9 lots within the 500 foot radius of
the council member's home. These designations appear to affect certain identifiable
parcels of land, therefore Councilmember Rindone does not meet all of the requirements
of 18704.2(b)(3).

Thus based on the information you provided, it does not appear that the general
plan exception is applicable to your facts.⁴ Councilmember Rindone's property is
deemed "directly involved" in the general plan decision.

5. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon the council
member's economic interests will meet the applicable materiality standard?

If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard in regulation 18705.2(a)
applies. (Regulation 18704.2(c)(1).)

Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property (other than a leasehold) which is directly involved in a
governmental decision is presumed to be material. This presumption is rebutted by proof

⁴ Please note, however, that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
assistance; This assistance is based solely on the facts you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
71.)

File No. A-05-057
Page No. 8

that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect – even a penny's worth – on the official's real property. (Ibid.)

Thus, it is presumed that the financial effect of a governmental decision on
Councilmember's private residence, which is directly involved in the
governmental decision, is material. Therefore, unless this presumption is rebutted, the
council member is disqualified from participating in the general plan decisions.

An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby, supra.) Therefore, the determination of whether or not it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the council member's
real property is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for him to decide.

7. & 8. The public generally and legally required participation exceptions.

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.

The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.)

You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
Councilmember Rindone's situation, therefore we do not address them here.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

the plan is adopted. Lastly, you stated that the decisions also do not concern identifiable
parcels of land as the land use concepts (A-C) described in your earlier letter of
November 17,2003, were general in nature and not specific. You also said in the
telephone call that the general plan update does.not concern the agency,s prior,
concurrent or subsequent approval of or change to, apermit licensg zoringdesignation,
zoning variancg land use ordinance, specific plan or its equivalent.
However, based on the information provided in your earlier letter, it appears that
the general plan proposed changes (Concepts A, B, and C) to properties within 500 feet
of Councilmember Rindone's primary residencg relate to identifiable parcels of land. In
your previous letter, you included a rnap which designates specific areas for the proposed
changes. Furthermore, you stated in that letter that Concept A, which would allow for
4A%o offices and 6OYo retail development, would affect 4properties within the 500 foot
radius. Concept B, which would allow 40o/o development as residential,4OYo as office
and 20Yo as retail, would affect 9 lots; and Concept C, would allow development of 4oyo
residential,soyo office and l|Yo retail, would affect 9 lots within the 500 foot radius of
the council member's home. These designations appearto affect certain identifiable
parcels of land, therefore Councilmember Rindone does not mwt all of the requirements
of 18704.2OX3).
Thus based on the information yoq provided, it does not appear that the general
plan exception is applicable to your facts.a Councilmember Rindone's property is
deemed "directly involved" in the general plan decision.
5. \ilhat is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon the council
member's economic interests will meet the applicable materiality standard?
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a govemmental decision, the materiality standard in regulation 18705.2(a)
applies. (Regulation 187 Oa.2@)(l).)
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental
decision on real property (other than a leasehold) which is directly involved in a
govemmental decision is presumed to be material. This presumption is rebutted by proof
o Please note, however, ttrat the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
assistance; This assistance is based solely on the facts you provide. (In re Ogtesby (1975) i fffC Ops.
71.)
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have any
financial effect - even a penny's worth - on the official's real property. (Ibid.)
Thus, it is presumed that the financial effect of a governmental decision on
CouncilmemberRindone's private residence, which is directly involved in the
goveflrmental decision, is material. Thereforg unless this presumption is rebutted, the
council member is disqualified from participating in the general plan decisions.
An effect upon economic interests is considetred "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. Qn re
Ogksby, supra.) Therefore, the determination of whether or not it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the council member,s
real property is necessaily a factual question that is ultimately forhim to decide.
7. & 8. The public generally and legally required participation exceptions.
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally'' exception- This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101 regulation 1S70S)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
Councilmember Rindone's situation, therefore we do not address them here.

QUESTION: May Councilmember Rindone participate in his official capacity, in community
meetings, informational sessions and/or city council workshops or hearings regarding ihe
city's general plan update?

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the govenrmental decisions
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Rindone,s
private residence. Therefore, unless this presumption is rebutted, or an exception under
regulation 18702.4 applies, the council member is disqualified from participating in the
general plan decisions.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 21, 2005 Elizabeth Wagner Hull Assistant City Attorney City of Chula Vista 276 Fourth Avenue Chula Vista, CA 91910 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-05-057 Dear Ms. Wagner: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Councilmember Jerry Rindone of the City of Chula Vista, for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTION May Councilmember Rindone participate in his official capacity, in community meetings, informational sessions and/or city council workshops or hearings regarding the city's general plan update? CONCLUSION Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the governmental decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Rindone's private residence. Therefore, unless this presumption is rebutted, or an exception under regulation 18702.4 applies, the council member is disqualified from participating in the general plan decisions. ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-05-057 Page No. 2 FACTS You are seeking advice regarding recent amendments to regulation 18704.2 regarding the general plan adoption or amendments. In a phone call, you referred to an earlier advice letter you received, Full Advice Letter A-03-280, dated January 6, 2004. This letter concluded that Councilmember Jerry Rindone was presumed to be materially affected by the proposed changes to the land use element of the city's general plan. The letter concluded that unless rebutted, the council member was disqualified from participating in the decision. Since that letter was written, the Commission has amended regulation 18704.2. You requested that facts from your earlier letter be incorporated in your current request for advice. You stated that none of the facts have changed. In your earlier letter, you said that Councilmember Rindone resides on the west side of the city and the city is evaluating the existing land use designations. The general plan proposes no change to his primary residence. However, the plan does propose possible changes to properties within 500 feet of it. The possible changes vary among three different land use concepts. One of the proposed concept plans will affect approximately 9 properties within that radius. The concepts are still very broad and it is too early in the process to more specifically identify the impacts or the number of parcels citywide subject to the same types of redesignation. There are three concepts under consideration: Concept A: Change from current "Retail Commercial" to proposed "Mixed Use Commercial" for 4 properties. The Mixed Use Commercial land would allow for 40% offices and 60% retail. Concept B: Change from current "Retail Commercial," "High Density Residential (18-27 du/ac)" and "Professional and Administrative Commercial" to proposed "Mixed Use." This change affects 9 lots within the 500 foot radius. This Mixed Use would allow 40% developed as residential (40 du/ac), 40% as office and 20% as retail. Concept
